questions.q: What is the difference between stack and heap memory allocation?
options: Stack stores local variables in a LIFO structure with automatic deallocation; heap stores dynamic objects managed by the program or GC
Stack is for large objects; heap is for small ones
Heap is faster than stack because it is randomly accessed
Both stack and heap have identical performance characteristics
questions.explanation: Stack allocation is fast (simple pointer increment) and automatic (exiting scope deallocates). Heap requires explicit allocation/deallocation (C) or garbage collection (Java). Stack is small/limited; heap is large. Stack is safe for temporary objects; heap for objects with unknown or dynamic lifetimes.

questions.q: In tracing garbage collection, what is a 'GC root', and why is it important?
options: A GC root is an object definitely reachable (stack variables, globals, registers); marking starts from roots and reachable objects are kept
A GC root is the oldest object in the heap
GC roots prevent garbage collection from ever running
The number of GC roots has no impact on garbage collection performance
questions.explanation: Tracing GC identifies reachable objects by traversing from roots (locals, globals, registers). Objects reachable from roots are live; unreachable ones are garbage. The garbage collector marks reachable objects, sweeps unmarked ones. Efficient root identification and marking is critical to GC speed.

questions.q: What is the difference between mark-and-sweep and generational garbage collection?
options: Mark-and-sweep marks all live objects then sweeps dead ones; generational assumes young objects die quickly, collecting young and old generations separately
Generational GC is just a renamed version of mark-and-sweep with no practical difference
Mark-and-sweep is faster than generational because it scans the entire heap at once
Both approaches require manual memory deallocation by the programmer
questions.explanation: Mark-and-sweep traverses the entire heap each collection, expensive for large heaps. Generational GC (Java, Python) assumes most objects die young; collect young generation frequently, old generation rarely. This reduces pause times and improves cache locality. Trade-off: more complex logic for better performance.

questions.q: What is 'reference counting', and what is a critical limitation of this garbage collection approach?
options: Reference counting tracks object references; when count reaches zero, the object is deallocated; limitation: circular references prevent deallocation
Reference counting is identical to mark-and-sweep and has no disadvantages
Reference counting never causes performance overhead because it is automatic
Reference counting only works with immutable objects
questions.explanation: Each object has a refcount; incrementing on reference, decrementing on release. Refcount=0 → deallocate. Swift and Python use reference counting (with cycle detection). Limitation: circular references (A → B → A) prevent deallocation, creating memory leaks. Requires periodic cycle detection.

questions.q: What is the Rust concept of 'ownership', and how does it replace garbage collection?
options: Ownership means a value has one owner; moving ownership transfers it; dropping the owner deallocates the value; no GC needed
Ownership is an alternative name for garbage collection with identical semantics
Ownership requires manual memory management exactly like C malloc/free
Ownership only applies to primitive types, not complex data structures
questions.explanation: Rust's ownership system enforces at compile time that values are freed exactly once when their owner goes out of scope. Moving ownership transfers responsibility. Borrowing (references) allows temporary use without ownership. This guarantees memory safety without GC overhead, achieving C-level performance with Java-level safety.

questions.q: What is 'write barriers' in garbage collection, and why are they used in generational collectors?
options: Write barriers are runtime checks tracking references from old to young generation objects, so young GC doesn't miss objects referenced from old generation
Write barriers are memory barriers in multicore systems preventing instruction reordering
Write barriers eliminate the need for marking objects in tracing GC
Write barriers are only relevant to single-threaded programs
questions.explanation: Generational GC collects young generation frequently without scanning the entire old generation. Write barriers intercept store operations, recording if a young object is referenced from old. These card marks tell the collector which old regions to scan. Without them, young GC would miss live objects in old generation.

questions.q: What does 'escape analysis' in Rust enable regarding memory allocation?
options: Escape analysis determines if references escape a scope; if not, stack allocation is safe and preferred over heap
Escape analysis is used to detect memory leaks in garbage-collected languages
Escape analysis is a debugging technique with no effect on performance or safety
Escape analysis prevents all heap allocations, forcing stack use everywhere
questions.explanation: Rust's borrow checker uses escape analysis to verify references don't outlive their data. If a reference doesn't escape (not returned, not stored globally), the referred data can be stack-allocated safely. This avoids heap allocation overhead. Programmers can hint with `&` (borrow) vs ownership.

questions.q: What is a 'memory barrier' or 'memory fence', and why do they matter in concurrent programming?
options: Memory barriers prevent CPU reordering of memory operations, ensuring visibility of changes across threads; critical for locks and atomics
Memory barriers are only relevant to garbage collectors, not concurrent code
Memory barriers prevent all memory access and eliminate performance entirely
Memory barriers are purely a theoretical concept with no practical runtime effect
questions.explanation: Modern CPUs reorder memory operations for performance. Memory barriers enforce order: LoadLoad, LoadStore, StoreStore, StoreLoad. In concurrent code, barriers ensure changes are visible to other threads. Mutexes and atomics use barriers. Weak (ARM) vs strong (x86) memory models affect barrier costs.

questions.q: What is the difference between 'manual memory management' and 'automatic memory management'?
options: Manual (C): programmer calls malloc/free; automatic (Java, Python): language handles allocation/deallocation via GC or ownership
Manual and automatic management produce identical performance and memory safety
Manual memory management is always safer because the programmer has direct control
Automatic memory management is impossible for any systems programming language
questions.explanation: Manual memory management (C, C++) requires explicit malloc/free; programmer errors cause leaks or use-after-free. Automatic management (Java, Python, Rust) handles deallocation via GC or ownership, eliminating memory safety errors at a performance cost. Rust's ownership is automatic with zero-cost abstraction.

questions.q: What is 'object pooling', and when is it beneficial in memory management?
options: Object pooling pre-allocates frequently-allocated objects to avoid repeated allocation/deallocation overhead, beneficial for latency-critical code
Object pooling is only used in languages without garbage collection
Object pooling eliminates garbage collection entirely and replaces it
Object pooling guarantees better cache locality than dynamic allocation
questions.explanation: Allocation and deallocation (or GC pauses) are expensive. Object pooling reuses pre-allocated objects, avoiding these costs. Useful in real-time systems (games, trading), thread pools, and latency-sensitive code. Trade-off: more memory usage, less GC pressure. Most languages benefit; languages with cheap allocation (Lua, modern GCs) matter less.
